threats.title: Complete Access Control Bypass via No-Op Security.sol Modifiers
threats.hypothesis_description: The onlyOwner and onlyManager modifiers in Security.sol evaluate boolean expressions as statement expressions (`msg.sender == owner;` and `managers[msg.sender] == true;`) without require() or revert(). These are valid Solidity syntax but have zero effect — they never revert, making every protected function callable by any address. This breaks all access control for Curves.sol (setFeeRedistributor, setProtocolFeePercent, setExternalFeePercent, setMaxFeePercent, setERC20Factory) and FeeSplitter.sol (addFees, onBalanceChange).
threats.investigation_rationale: This is the root cause for multiple downstream exploits. If modifiers truly don't revert, every privileged function is effectively public, enabling complete protocol takeover.

proof_of_signal.pattern: modifier onlyOwner() { msg.sender == owner; _; } — expression statement without require/revert, evaluates to bool and discards result

preconditions: No preconditions — any address can exploit immediately

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Modifiers use expression statements instead of require statements, yielding no-op access control
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: All admin, owner, and manager functions are gated solely by these broken modifiers

operational_questions: Compile and confirm that the modifier does not revert for non-owner callers
Does the Solidity 0.8.7 compiler emit any warning for this pattern?
Is there any external monitoring that would detect unauthorized calls?
validation_requirements: Deploy Security.sol, call transferOwnership from a non-owner address, confirm it succeeds
Call setManager from non-owner, confirm manager is set
Call all onlyOwner/onlyManager functions from random address

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Call transferOwnership(attackerAddress) on Curves contract to become owner
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call setManager(attackerAddress, true) to get manager role
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Use owner/manager privileges to redirect fees, change factory, change FeeSplitter
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Extract all protocol value via redirected fees and malicious contract injection
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None — single transaction by any account

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Complete protocol takeover — ownership and all admin roles
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All fee routing, token factory, and fee distributor can be replaced
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires redeployment — no mechanism to recover once ownership is stolen
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Every privileged operation in the protocol

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Monitor transferOwnership and setManager calls from non-owner addresses
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Fee destination changes, factory changes, FeeSplitter changes
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Rapid sequence of admin calls from unknown addresses

threats.title: FeeSplitter Total Drain via Unprotected setCurves() Pointing to Malicious Contract
threats.hypothesis_description: FeeSplitter.setCurves() is a public function with NO access control modifier at all. Anyone can call it to replace the curves reference with a malicious contract. Since FeeSplitter.balanceOf() and totalSupply() read live data from curves, an attacker can deploy a contract returning fabricated balances (e.g., attacker has 100% of supply) then call claimFees to drain all ETH from FeeSplitter.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a direct path to draining all holder fees. The function has zero protection and controls the trust root for all fee calculations.

proof_of_signal.pattern: function setCurves(Curves curves_) public { curves = curves_; } — no onlyOwner, no onlyManager, no access control at all

preconditions: FeeSplitter holds ETH from accumulated holder fees
No preconditions on caller — any address can execute

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: setCurves has zero access control, directly controls the data source for all fee calculations
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent — exploitable whenever FeeSplitter has ETH balance

operational_questions: How much ETH accumulates in FeeSplitter over time?
Can the attack be executed atomically in a single transaction?
Does setCurves need to be called before or after fee accumulation?
validation_requirements: Deploy malicious Curves mock returning attacker as sole holder with large balance
Call setCurves with mock address
Call claimFees to extract all ETH

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Deploy malicious contract implementing curvesTokenBalance and curvesTokenSupply, returning attacker address as having all tokens
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Call claimFees for a token that has accumulated fees — getClaimableFees will use fabricated balances
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: All ETH in FeeSplitter is sent to attacker

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Complete control of fee distribution trust root
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All holder fees drained, legitimate holders lose all accumulated fees

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Trivial — single transaction with prepared contract

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Monitor setCurves calls and curves address changes
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Curves reference pointing to non-legitimate address
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: setCurves called followed immediately by claimFees

threats.title: Fee Theft via Token Transfer Without FeeSplitter Notification
threats.hypothesis_description: The _transfer function in Curves.sol (used by transferCurvesToken and transferAllCurvesTokens) does not call feeRedistributor.onBalanceChange() for either sender or receiver. The receiving address has userFeeOffset of 0 by default. When the receiver calls claimFees, getClaimableFees computes owed = (cumulativeFeePerToken - 0) * balance, effectively claiming ALL historical fees for that token from inception. This drains the FeeSplitter of ETH that belongs to other holders.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is an on-chain exploitable fee theft. Any token holder can transfer to a fresh address and claim retroactive fees they never earned, directly violating the fee distribution invariant.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _transfer updates curvesTokenBalance for from/to but never calls feeRedistributor.onBalanceChange — receiver's userFeeOffset stays at default 0

preconditions: Token has accumulated holder fees (cumulativeFeePerToken > 0)
Attacker holds at least 1 curves token for the subject
FeeSplitter has ETH balance

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: _transfer is a pure balance update with no FeeSplitter interaction; receiver's fee offset remains 0
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None — any token holder can execute
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time after fees have been distributed for a token

operational_questions: How much ETH can be drained per token based on cumulativeFeePerToken?
Can the attacker repeat this with multiple fresh addresses?
Does the existing onBalanceChange call on buys partially mitigate this?
validation_requirements: Buy tokens, generate fees via trades, transfer to fresh address, call claimFees from fresh address
Verify claimable amount equals historical total rather than proportional share
Test repeated transfer-claim cycle

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify a token with high cumulativeFeePerToken (many trades have occurred)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Buy or hold tokens for that subject
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Transfer tokens to a fresh address (address with userFeeOffset = 0)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: From fresh address, call claimFees — claims all historical fees proportional to transferred balance

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: FeeSplitter drained of ETH, legitimate holders cannot claim their earned fees
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All fee claiming for all tokens

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: Any amount of curves tokens for the target subject

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Large claimFees calls from addresses that recently received transfers
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: FeeSplitter balance dropping faster than fee income

threats.title: Protocol Fee Redirection and Fee Manipulation via Broken Access Control
threats.hypothesis_description: Due to the no-op modifiers in Security.sol, anyone can call setProtocolFeePercent to set protocolFeeDestination to their own address, setMaxFeePercent to increase the maximum, and setExternalFeePercent to maximize all fee percentages. An attacker can set fees to near 100%, making every subsequent buy route almost all ETH to the attacker's address. Additionally, setting fees > price causes underflow in sell calculations, permanently trapping sellers.
threats.investigation_rationale: Combined with OPS-H001, this enables complete extraction of all future protocol trade value. The fee validation logic trusts maxFeePercent which is itself settable by anyone.

proof_of_signal.pattern: setProtocolFeePercent uses onlyOwner (no-op), validates sum <= maxFeePercent; setMaxFeePercent uses onlyManager (no-op), validates current sum <= new max. Attacker sets maxFeePercent high first, then sets fees.

preconditions: No preconditions — any address can call all functions

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Attacker chain: setMaxFeePercent(1 ether) → setProtocolFeePercent(0.99 ether, attackerAddr) → all buys route 99% to attacker
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None — broken modifiers make this permissionless

operational_questions: Can fees be set high enough to cause sell underflow (totalFee > price)?
Does setProtocolFeePercent correctly validate destination != address(0)?
Can this be combined with setERC20Factory to deploy backdoored tokens?
validation_requirements: Set maxFeePercent to 1 ether, then set protocolFeePercent to 0.99 ether with attacker address
Attempt a buy and verify fee routing to attacker
Attempt a sell and verify underflow revert when totalFee > price

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Call setMaxFeePercent(type(uint256).max) — no revert due to broken onlyManager
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call setProtocolFeePercent(0.95 ether, attackerAddress) — routes 95% of buy fees to attacker
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: All subsequent buys route 95%+ of the price as protocol fee to attacker
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Optionally set fees >= 1 ether to make sells revert (sellValue underflow), trapping user tokens

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All future trades route fees to attacker; sells permanently broken if fees > 100%
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires redeployment; trapped funds in existing tokens may be unrecoverable

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Fee parameter changes from non-owner addresses
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Sudden fee increases or destination changes
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: setMaxFeePercent followed by setProtocolFeePercent from same address

threats.title: Permanent Fee Loss via onBalanceChange Overwriting Offset Without Crediting Accrued Fees
threats.hypothesis_description: FeeSplitter.onBalanceChange() sets data.userFeeOffset[account] = data.cumulativeFeePerToken without first calling updateFeeCredit(). This means all fees accumulated by the user since their last offset update are permanently destroyed. This happens on EVERY buy via _transferFees which calls onBalanceChange. Additionally, since onlyManager is broken, any attacker can call onBalanceChange(token, victimAddress) to wipe any user's accumulated unclaimed fees.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is both a passive bug (fees lost on every buy) and an actively exploitable griefing attack (wipe anyone's fees). The fee calculation in updateFeeCredit correctly computes owed fees, but onBalanceChange bypasses it entirely.

proof_of_signal.pattern: onBalanceChange sets data.userFeeOffset[account] = data.cumulativeFeePerToken without calling updateFeeCredit first — (cumulativeFeePerToken - oldOffset) * balance worth of fees permanently lost

preconditions: User has accumulated unclaimed fees (cumulativeFeePerToken > userFeeOffset[user])
For active exploit: no preconditions due to broken onlyManager

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: onBalanceChange is called during every _transferFees (every buy), resetting the buyer's offset and destroying their accumulated fees
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None for active exploit — broken onlyManager allows anyone to call
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent — passive loss on every buy, active attack anytime

operational_questions: How much ETH is lost per user per buy transaction?
Does the lost ETH become claimable by other users or is it permanently orphaned?
Can an attacker profit by wiping others' fees and then claiming a larger share?
validation_requirements: Buy token, wait for trades to generate fees, verify claimable > 0, buy again, verify claimable reset to 0
From external address, call onBalanceChange for a user with accumulated fees, verify fees wiped

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify users with large unclaimed fee balances
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call onBalanceChange(token, victimAddress) for each victim — their offset resets, fees are lost
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Attacker's relative share of future fees increases as others' entitlements are destroyed
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Claim the increased share of fees

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All users lose accumulated fees on every buy; active griefing wipes arbitrary users' fees
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All fee claiming, all buy operations

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: None for griefing; any amount for profit from increased share

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Users reporting lost fees after buying more tokens
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: External onBalanceChange calls targeting high-fee users

threats.title: Trading DoS via ERC20 Wrapping Reducing FeeSplitter totalSupply to Zero
threats.hypothesis_description: FeeSplitter.totalSupply() computes (curvesTokenSupply - curvesTokenBalance[token][address(curves)]) * PRECISION, excluding tokens locked in the Curves contract (wrapped as ERC20). If all circulating tokens for a subject are withdrawn to ERC20, totalSupply becomes 0. FeeSplitter.addFees() reverts on totalSupply == 0 (NoTokenHolders). Since _transferFees calls addFees when holdersFeePercent > 0, ALL buy and sell operations for that token will revert, permanently halting trading.
threats.investigation_rationale: A single token holder can DoS an entire token's trading by wrapping all tokens to ERC20. The only recovery is if someone deposits tokens back, but the subject's first token (which can never be sold) is still counted in supply — need to check if the subject can withdraw their token.

proof_of_signal.pattern: totalSupply = (supply - curves.curvesTokenBalance(token, address(curves))) * PRECISION; addFees reverts if totalSupply_ == 0

preconditions: holdersFeePercent > 0 (holder fees enabled)
Attacker holds enough tokens to bring circulating supply to match locked amount
All non-attacker holders also withdraw, or attacker holds all circulating tokens

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: withdraw() moves internal tokens to contract and mints ERC20, increasing locked balance and decreasing totalSupply in FeeSplitter
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None — any token holder can withdraw

operational_questions: Can the token subject withdraw their 1 mandatory token?
If supply=2 and both are withdrawn, does totalSupply hit 0?
Is there a recovery mechanism besides depositing tokens back?
validation_requirements: Create token with supply=2, withdraw both to ERC20, attempt to buy — verify revert
Verify addFees reverts when totalSupply is 0
Test if depositing 1 token back restores trading

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Buy all available tokens for a target subject (or acquire majority)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call withdraw() for all held tokens — internal balance moves to Curves contract
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: totalSupply in FeeSplitter drops to 0 (or near 0)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: All buyCurvesToken and sellCurvesToken calls revert because _transferFees → addFees reverts

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete trading halt for the target token
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires someone to deposit ERC20 back, but attacker controls the ERC20 tokens
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All buy/sell for the targeted token subject

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: Enough curves tokens to bring totalSupply to 0
execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Moderate — may need to be sole holder

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Large withdraw operations for specific tokens
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Trading failing for previously active tokens

threats.title: Protocol Fee and Referral Fee Permanently Locked in Curves Contract on Sells
threats.hypothesis_description: In _transferFees during sells, the sellValue sent to the seller is computed as price - protocolFee - subjectFee - referralFee - holderFee. The protocolFee is deducted from the seller's payout but never forwarded to protocolFeeDestination (only the sellValue goes to firstDestination which is msg.sender on sells). Similarly, when no referral is defined, the referralFee is deducted from sellValue but the transfer is skipped (returns (true, bytes(''))), permanently locking both amounts in the Curves contract. There is no withdrawal function for this locked ETH.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is an accounting error in the sell fee distribution. ETH accumulates permanently in the Curves contract with no extraction mechanism, representing a continual loss.

proof_of_signal.pattern: For sells: firstDestination=msg.sender, sellValue=price-protocolFee-subjectFee-referralFee-holderFee sent to seller. protocolFee is deducted but never sent anywhere on sells. referralFee skipped if no referral defined.

preconditions: protocolFeePercent > 0 (protocol fee is configured)
Any sell transaction occurs

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: On buy: protocolFee sent to protocolFeeDestination. On sell: protocolFee deducted from seller but never forwarded — asymmetric handling
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None — happens automatically on every sell

operational_questions: How much ETH has been locked from sells since deployment?
Is there any admin function to extract locked ETH from Curves?
Does this affect contract solvency for future sells?
validation_requirements: Execute a sell, trace ETH flow, verify protocolFee stays in contract
Verify no receive/fallback/withdraw function in Curves for extracting trapped ETH
Calculate cumulative ETH loss over protocol lifetime

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Not directly exploitable for profit, but causes permanent ETH leakage
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Over time, Curves contract accumulates ETH from all sell protocolFees
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Eventually, contract may have insufficient ETH for sell payouts if bonding curve backing erodes
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: No recovery — ETH is permanently locked with no extraction function
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None — systemic issue on every sell

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Continuous ETH leakage; protocol loses revenue from all sells; potential long-term insolvency
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All sell operations, protocol fee collection

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Curves contract ETH balance growing beyond expected bonding curve reserves
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Protocol fee destination not receiving fees from sells
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Increasing contract balance discrepancy over time

threats.title: Fee Theft via Withdraw/Deposit Cycle Exploiting Missing FeeSplitter Updates
threats.hypothesis_description: Neither withdraw() nor deposit() in Curves.sol calls feeRedistributor.onBalanceChange(). When a user withdraws tokens, their internal balance decreases (moving to address(this)), but their userFeeOffset in FeeSplitter remains unchanged. The FeeSplitter's totalSupply decreases (fewer circulating tokens). New fees distributed during this period go to fewer holders at a higher per-token rate. When the user deposits back, their balance is restored, and getClaimableFees computes (cumulativeFeePerToken - oldOffset) * restoredBalance, including fees from the period when the user held 0 internal tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: This allows a user to earn fees for periods where they did not hold internal tokens. The fee rate is amplified by reduced totalSupply, creating a profitable arbitrage cycle.

proof_of_signal.pattern: withdraw calls _transfer(subject, msg.sender, address(this), amount) without calling feeRedistributor.onBalanceChange — userFeeOffset stays stale, totalSupply changes

preconditions: User holds curves tokens
ERC20 token is deployed for the subject
Active trading generates holder fees between withdraw and deposit

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: withdraw moves tokens to contract custody without FeeSplitter notification; deposit moves them back — user's offset remains unchanged throughout, capturing fees for inactive period
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Between withdraw and deposit, while trading fees accumulate

operational_questions: How much excess fee can be captured per cycle?
Does the amplified per-token rate from reduced totalSupply make this more profitable?
Can this be repeated multiple times?
validation_requirements: Hold tokens, note claimable fees, withdraw, wait for trades, deposit, check claimable — should include fees from wrapped period
Compare fee earned vs fee earned by a holder who stayed in internal tokens
Verify FeeSplitter solvency after multiple cycles

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Hold N internal tokens with userFeeOffset at C0
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call withdraw(subject, N) — internal balance drops to 0, ERC20 minted, offset unchanged
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Wait for trading to generate fees — cumulativeFeePerToken increases at amplified rate
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Call deposit(subject, N * 1e18) — internal balance restored to N
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None — single user, timing based on trade volume

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: FeeSplitter becomes insolvent as users claim more than deposited
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Fee distribution for all holders of affected tokens

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Withdraw followed by deposit for the same user and token
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: FeeSplitter balance decreasing faster than expected
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Cyclic withdraw-deposit patterns timed around high-volume trading

threats.title: Unauthorized ERC20 Token Deployment via withdraw() Bypassing onlyTokenSubject
threats.hypothesis_description: The withdraw() function calls _deployERC20() directly when no ERC20 token exists yet, bypassing the _mint() function which has the onlyTokenSubject modifier. Any holder can trigger ERC20 deployment with default name 'Curves' and symbol 'CURVES' (or whatever was pre-set). Once deployed, the subject can never change the name/symbol since ERC20TokenAlreadyMinted would revert on subsequent mint() calls.
threats.investigation_rationale: This violates the subject's exclusive right to control ERC20 deployment and naming. A griefing attacker can lock in unfavorable metadata by being the first to withdraw.

proof_of_signal.pattern: withdraw checks if externalToken == address(0), then calls _deployERC20 directly — _deployERC20 has no access control, _mint has onlyTokenSubject but is bypassed

preconditions: ERC20 token not yet deployed for the subject
Attacker holds at least 1 curves token for the subject

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: withdraw() contains inline ERC20 deployment logic that bypasses the subject-only gate on _mint()

operational_questions: Can the subject still set name/symbol before withdrawal if they act quickly?
Does deploying with default CURVES symbol conflict with other tokens?
Is there a way to redeploy with correct metadata?
validation_requirements: Buy 1 token as non-subject, call withdraw before subject calls mint, verify ERC20 deploys with defaults
Verify subject cannot call setNameAndSymbol or mint after withdrawal-triggered deployment

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Buy tokens from a subject who hasn't deployed their ERC20 yet
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call withdraw() — triggers _deployERC20 with default or pre-set metadata
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Subject's mint() will revert with ERC20TokenAlreadyMinted
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: ERC20 token is permanently deployed with potentially undesired metadata

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Subject loses control over ERC20 metadata
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: ERC20 token naming and identity for the subject

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: 1 curves token for the target subject

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: withdraw() calls that trigger ERC20 deployment for subjects that haven't called mint()
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: ERC20 tokens with default CURVES naming

threats.title: Arithmetic Underflow in getPrice Prevents Multi-Token Initial Purchase
threats.hypothesis_description: getPrice(supply=0, amount>1) causes an arithmetic underflow revert in Solidity 0.8.7. The sum2 calculation uses (supply - 1 + amount) which evaluates supply-1 first. When supply=0, this underflows (0-1) and reverts due to checked arithmetic. This prevents subjects from buying more than 1 token in their initial buyCurvesTokenForPresale or buyCurvesTokenWithName call.
threats.investigation_rationale: This forces the subject to buy exactly 1 token initially. Any subsequent purchase is at a different (higher) price and exposed to other buyers who can see the new supply on-chain, reducing the subject's ability to bootstrap their curve.

proof_of_signal.pattern: sum2 computation: (supply - 1 + amount) * (supply + amount) * (2 * (supply - 1 + amount) + 1) / 6 — supply-1 underflows when supply=0 in Solidity 0.8.7 checked arithmetic

preconditions: supply == 0 (new curve being created)
amount > 1

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: getPrice formula uses supply-1 without checking supply >= 1, causing underflow for initial multi-token purchase

operational_questions: Is there a workaround in the frontend/SDK?
Does forcing 1-token initial purchase create exploitable timing windows?
Does the sum1 calculation also underflow?
validation_requirements: Call getPrice(0, 2) and verify it reverts
Call getPrice(0, 1) and verify it returns 0 (first token free)
Verify buyCurvesTokenWithName with amount=5 reverts

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Forces subjects to buy 1 token at a time from supply=0
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Second purchase at supply=1 is observable on-chain, creating information asymmetry
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: On chains with public mempools (not this chain), enables sandwich attacks on subject's second buy

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Subjects cannot bootstrap curves with multiple tokens atomically
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: N/A — workaround is to buy 1 token then buy more

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: All initial purchases are exactly amount=1

threats.title: Excess ETH from Buy Overpayment Permanently Locked in Curves Contract
threats.hypothesis_description: _buyCurvesToken checks msg.value >= price + totalFee but never refunds the excess. Any ETH beyond the exact required amount stays permanently locked in the Curves contract with no withdrawal mechanism. This is especially dangerous for smart contract integrations that estimate prices before sending transactions, as price can change between estimation and execution.
threats.investigation_rationale: Users and integrating contracts will routinely overpay due to price estimation, permanently losing the excess ETH. There is no recovery function in the contract.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (msg.value < price + totalFee) revert InsufficientPayment(); — only checks minimum, excess msg.value stays in contract, no refund logic

preconditions: User sends more ETH than price + totalFee

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Only >= check on msg.value, no refund of excess, no receive/withdraw function for extracting trapped ETH
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None — user error or estimate mismatch
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Every buy transaction where msg.value > exact amount

operational_questions: How likely is overpayment in typical usage patterns?
Is there an admin function to sweep locked ETH? (No)
Could this accumulate to significant amounts?
validation_requirements: Send 2x the required ETH for a buy, verify excess stays in contract
Confirm no withdrawal or sweep function exists in Curves

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: N/A — not directly exploitable for profit by attacker
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Users naturally overpay when price changes between estimation and execution

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Curves contract balance exceeding expected bonding curve reserves
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Contract balance growing disproportionate to trading volume

threats.title: FeeSplitter Duplicate userTokens Array Enables Gas Griefing and Potential Fee Double-Counting in batchClaiming
threats.hypothesis_description: FeeSplitter.onBalanceChange() unconditionally pushes the token to userTokens[account] whenever balance > 0, without checking for duplicates. Since onBalanceChange is called on every buy (and is callable by anyone due to broken onlyManager), the userTokens array grows unboundedly with duplicate entries. This causes: (1) getUserTokensAndClaimable and batchClaiming to consume excessive gas, potentially causing out-of-gas reverts; (2) batchClaiming iterates duplicates — updateFeeCredit is called multiple times per token, but after the first call zeros the offset, subsequent calls for the same token within the same batch may miscalculate or produce zero, making the batch result unreliable.
threats.investigation_rationale: The unbounded duplicate array growth is a direct DoS vector. An attacker can call onBalanceChange(token, victimAddress) repeatedly to bloat any user's array, permanently degrading their ability to batch-claim fees.

proof_of_signal.pattern: onBalanceChange: if (balanceOf(token, account) > 0) userTokens[account].push(token); — no duplicate check, push on every call

preconditions: User has balance > 0 for a token
onBalanceChange is called multiple times (via buys or direct calls due to broken access control)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Every buy pushes duplicate to userTokens. With broken onlyManager, attacker can call onBalanceChange in a loop to rapidly bloat any user's array.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None — broken onlyManager allows anyone to call

operational_questions: How many duplicates accumulate per buy?
At what array size does batchClaiming start failing with out-of-gas?
Can batchClaiming double-count fees due to processing the same token twice?
validation_requirements: Buy token 10 times, check userTokens length — should be 10 (all same token)
Call onBalanceChange directly 1000 times for victim, attempt batchClaiming with getUserTokens result
Verify if batchClaiming sends more ETH than it should due to duplicate processing

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify target user with claimable fees
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call onBalanceChange(token, victim) in a loop (e.g., 10000 times) — possible due to broken onlyManager
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Victim's userTokens array grows to 10000+ entries
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Victim's batchClaiming and getUserTokensAndClaimable calls fail with out-of-gas

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Targeted users cannot batch-claim fees; must use individual claimFees per token
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: No mechanism to shrink userTokens array — permanent degradation

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Repeated onBalanceChange calls from non-Curves addresses
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: High-frequency onBalanceChange calls for specific users
